rationale: The cited paper is used in the conceptual background to support a specific mechanism: as goal proximity increases, effort increases. The authors invoke Kivetz et al. (2006) to motivate their prediction that showing borrowers time-to-payoff information could change repayment motivation and effort. This is more than a passing literature list mention because it directly informs hypothesis framing for one of the focal disclosure interventions. The citing paper does not primarily build a full extension of the cited program-reward framework, but it clearly transfers that core idea to a debt-repayment setting.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the goal-proximity/goal-gradient idea from Kivetz et al. (2006) to credit card repayment decisions. It uses that prior finding to justify predictions about how time-to-payoff disclosures might increase or decrease repayment effort depending on perceived distance to payoff.
